q: Which statement correctly describes self-incompatibility in flowering plants?
options: Self-incompatibility prevents any pollen from germinating on the stigma of the same plant, promoting cross-pollination and genetic diversity
Self-incompatibility ensures that only pollen from the same plant can fertilize the ovule, guaranteeing seed production
Self-incompatibility is a mechanism where the pistil actively destroys all pollen grains that land on it
Self-incompatibility only occurs in wind-pollinated species to reduce wasted pollen
explanation: Self-incompatibility (SI) systems recognize and reject pollen carrying the same S-allele as the pistil, preventing self-fertilization and forcing cross-pollination, which increases genetic variation.
